title: Course setup
text: This course contains a few hands-on activities to aid understanding for some concepts we will cover. These have been designed to be carried out in R. To follow along, please make sure you have the following ready before coming to class.

title: Module 5: Practical interpretation
text:
Dimensionality reduction aids visualizations and insights into your data, but interpreting them correctly requires careful thought. This module focuses on how to critically evaluate results and avoid common pitfalls.
What you’ll explore:

How to interpret clustering and embedding plots

How preprocessing choices influence results

How to identify potential artifacts such as batch effects

What questions to ask when evaluating biological data

The full content for this module will be released on the morning of the workshop!

title: Exploratory analysis
text:
You’ve now gone through the entire course, congrats! If you’d like to further refine your understanding, complete the activities given here as homework.
This optional exercise allows you to apply the concepts from the workshop to a new dataset.
What you’ll explore:

How to approach a dataset from start to finish

How preprocessing affects results

How to choose appropriate methods

How to interpret outputs in a biological context

What to do now:

No preparation required

This activity can be completed during or after the workshop

Details for this exercise will be shared during the workshop!

title: About
text:
About this site

1 + 1

[1] 2

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Course description
This is an interactive pilot workshop aimed at strengthening foundational linear algebra concepts in the context of biological data analysis. The focus will largely be on gaining an intuitive understanding of how biological data is structured and transformed in modern analysis workflows, including clustering and dimensionality reduction methods like PCA, t-SNE, and UMAP. Participants will learn how to critically evaluate their data, choose appropriate analytical methods, and derive biologically meaningful interpretations. This course is aimed at researchers seeking a conceptual understanding of the framework typically implemented in analyzing biological data. Open to all skill levels.


Prerequisites
Intro to R I and Intro to Biostatistics I preferred but not required.



Learning objectives
By the end of this workshop, participants will be able to:

Represent biological data as vectors and matrices
Interpret similarity and distance between samples
Understand how transformations affect data structure
Understand the need for dimensionality reduction
Explain the biological significance of computing eigenvectors and eigenvalues
Distinguish between popularly used dimensionality reduction techniques
Identify appropriate methods/techniques for specific use cases
Critically evaluate plots in practice




Course structure
The course has been divided into 5 modules and an optional take-home activity as follows:
Module 1: Understanding biological data structure
Biological data is often high-dimensional and can be represented using linear algebraic structures such as vectors and matrices. This module introduces how to think about biological datasets in terms of samples, features, and similarity.
Module 2: Data transformations
Preprocessing steps such as normalization, centering, and scaling can substantially change how data is represented and interpreted. This module explores how these transformations impact data structure and downstream analysis.
Module 3: Dimensionality reduction
High-dimensional datasets are difficult to visualize and interpret directly. This module introduces dimensionality reduction using Principal Component Analysis (PCA), focusing on intuitive interpretation and the biological meaning of principal components.
Module 4: Beyond PCA
While PCA captures global structure, it may not fully resolve complex patterns in high-dimensional data. This module introduces nonlinear dimensionality reduction methods such as UMAP and t-SNE, and discusses how they differ from PCA.
Module 5: Practical interpretation
This module focuses on interpreting real-world results. Participants will learn how to evaluate plots, identify potential artifacts (e.g., batch effects), and determine which analytical methods are appropriate for different datasets.
Optional: Exploratory analysis
Participants can apply the concepts learned in the workshop to analyze a new dataset, exploring its structure and drawing informed conclusions about underlying biological patterns.

title: About the instructor
text:
Sonal Kumar
I’m a PhD Candidate in the collaborative Neuroscience program offered through Tufts University and the Jackson Laboratory. My research in the Carter lab at JAX integrates multiomics data with clinical information to better understand Alzheimer’s disease-associated neurodegeneration and functional decline with aging in a non-human primate model system. Outside of my research, I’m passionate about making computational biology intuitive and accessible for students!


Contact and feedback
If you have any questions or would like to chat about this course, please feel free to reach out at sonal [dot] kumar [at] jax [dot] org or raise an issue in the course GitHub repository.
Your suggestions are always welcome! If you are taking this course in May 2026, please consider filling out this form to provide feedback that can be integrated for improving subsequent offerings for this workshop.

title: Module 4: Expanding beyond the PCA
text:
While PCA is widely used, it may not always capture complex structure in high-dimensional data. Other methods, frequently using non-linear modeling, can highlight different aspects of the data.
What you’ll explore:

How PCA compares to methods like UMAP and t-SNE

The difference between global and local structure

How different methods represent patterns in the data

When different dimensionality reduction approaches are useful

The full content for this module will be released on the morning of the workshop!

title: Module 1: Understanding biological data structure
text:
Biological datasets are often high-dimensional and can be represented using familiar mathematical structures such as vectors and matrices. In this module, we will focus on how to interpret biological data in this framework and how to compare samples based on their features.
What you’ll explore:

What rows and columns represent in a biological dataset

How to think of samples (cells) as vectors

How similarity and distance between samples are defined

How clustering methods group similar samples

The full content for this module will be released on the morning of the workshop!

title: Module 3: Need for dimensionality reduction
text:
Biological datasets often contain thousands of features, making them difficult to visualize and interpret directly. Dimensionality reduction provides a way to summarize this information while preserving important patterns.
What you’ll explore:

Why dimensionality reduction is needed

How Principal Component Analysis (PCA) summarizes variation in data

How to interpret principal components in a biological context

How much variation is captured by each component

The full content for this module will be released on the morning of the workshop!

title: Course setup
text:
1. Environment

Ensure you have the latest compatible versions of RStudio and R installed for your computer. If you have not previously used R/RStudio, you can install them using the Self-Service desktop application or by reaching out directly to JAX IT.
Make sure you have the following packages installed. Simply copy the code chunk and run it in the RStudio console:


install.packages(c(
  "tidyverse",
  "factoextra",
  "Rtsne",
  "uwot"
))


To make sure the packages installed correctly, go ahead and try loading them in by running the following in the console:


library(tidyverse)
library(factoextra)
library(Rtsne)
library(uwot)

title: Course setup
text:
2. Data
We will be using the same dataset throughout the course. The original study sequenced 2,700 peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) from a healthy donor, which is freely available as the PBMC3k dataset from 10X Genomics.
We have generated a subset of this dataset for demonstration in this workshop. Briefly, we did xyz to the data. As the focus of the course is not on analyzing single cell RNASeq in R using Seurat, we’ve tried to retain a high-dimensional dataset that can still be analyzed easily locally on personal computers. More details on data preprocessing are given in this script.
Clicking on this link should start the download of the .rds file. This is a file type that allows reading in R objects directly, and are often better for reproducibility. Once downloaded, you can load your data in the workspace like so:

#load file in your workspace.
data <- readRDS("pbmc3k_subset.rds") #remember to confirm the path to your file.

#data is a list of many files, and individual files can be accessed like so:
pbmc3k <- data$readcounts
metadata <- data$metadata

title: Course setup
text:
3. Code
While individual course modules will be populated with relevant code that can be followed to reproduce results, it is recommended to download and work on this Quarto document for course participants. You will be using it throughout the workshop, and you can run through it line by line with the instructor and make any notes, if needed.
This is all that you need to get started!






Note



If you’d like to follow along with the class in real time, then please plan to come with these installed/downloaded. As this is a short workshop, we will not have time to troubleshoot software installations!

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
This notebook is designed to accompany the workshop content.
Do not spend the full workshop walking through every line of code.
Suggested use:

Run selected chunks live.
Use plots as teaching anchors.
Pause often for interpretation.
Skip optional chunks if running behind.

Main teaching story:

Biological data can be represented as a matrix.
Once data is a matrix, we can compare samples, transform the data, reduce dimensionality, and visualize structure.

Dataset: PBMC3k single-cell RNA-seq data.
We will treat:

cells as observations/samples
genes as features/variables
expression values as measurements

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Goal
By the end of this module, participants should understand:

biological data as a matrix
rows and columns as observations and features
cells as vectors
similarity and distance between cells

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Load dataset

options(timeout = 300) # Increases timeout to 5 minutes
SeuratData::InstallData("pbmc3k")


SeuratData::InstallData("pbmc3k")
pbmc <- SeuratData::LoadData("pbmc3k")

Instructor note:
Use a preprocessed PBMC3k object for the workshop. Avoid making participants run the full Seurat preprocessing workflow unless this is explicitly part of the course.

pbmc <- readRDS("data/pbmc3k_clean.rds")
pbmc

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Inspect metadata

head(pbmc@meta.data)


table(pbmc$seurat_annotations)

Instructor pause:
Ask:

What does one row in the metadata represent?
What kinds of information do we have about each cell?
Which variables are biological labels?
Which variables might be technical?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Extract expression matrix

expr <- GetAssayData(pbmc, assay = "RNA", layer = "data")
expr[1:5, 1:5]


dim(expr)

Instructor note:
Seurat stores this matrix as:
genes × cells
For most of our linear algebra intuition, it is easier to think in terms of:
cells × genes
So we transpose the matrix.

expr_t <- t(as.matrix(expr))
dim(expr_t)

Instructor pause:
Ask:

What does one row represent now?
What does one column represent now?
What is one cell represented as mathematically?

Answer:
Each cell is a vector of gene expression values.

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Select variable genes
Instructor note:
We do not need every gene for teaching. Using all genes slows things down and adds noise.

variable_genes <- VariableFeaturePlot(pbmc)[1:1000]
expr_var <- expr_t[, variable_genes]

dim(expr_var)

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
section: Visualize a small part of the matrix
text:
Visualize a small part of the matrix

expr_small <- expr_var[1:10, 1:10]
expr_small

Instructor pause:
Ask:

Is this a small matrix or a slice of a larger matrix?
What would a row vector mean?
What would a column vector mean?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Look at expression distribution

expr_long_sample <- expr_var[
  sample(rownames(expr_var), 300),
  sample(colnames(expr_var), 50)
] %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("cell") %>%
  pivot_longer(-cell, names_to = "gene", values_to = "expression")

ggplot(expr_long_sample, aes(x = expression)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of expression values",
    x = "Log-normalized expression",
    y = "Count"
  )

Instructor pause:
Ask:

Are most values high or low?
What does this suggest about biological data?
Is this matrix dense or sparse-ish?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Subset cells for distance calculations
Instructor note:
Pairwise distances across all cells can be slow and visually overwhelming. Use a subset.

cell_subset <- sample(rownames(expr_var), 250)
expr_subset <- expr_var[cell_subset, ]

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Compute distances between cells

cell_dist <- dist(expr_subset, method = "euclidean")
cell_dist_matrix <- as.matrix(cell_dist)

cell_dist_matrix[1:5, 1:5]

Instructor explanation:
Each value represents how far apart two cells are based on their gene expression vectors.
Instructor pause:
Ask:

What does a distance of 0 mean?
Why is the diagonal 0?
Why is the matrix symmetric?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Hierarchical clustering

hc <- hclust(cell_dist)

plot(hc, labels = FALSE, main = "Hierarchical clustering of cells")

Instructor pause:
Ask:

What is being clustered?
What information was used to cluster cells?
Do we know yet whether these clusters are biologically meaningful?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Heatmap of cell distances

heatmap(
  cell_dist_matrix,
  Rowv = as.dendrogram(hc),
  Colv = as.dendrogram(hc),
  scale = "none",
  labRow = FALSE,
  labCol = FALSE,
  main = "Cell-cell distance matrix"
)

Instructor note:
This is a good moment to connect:

similarity
distance
clustering
visual structure

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Goal
By the end of this module, participants should understand:

centering
scaling
why preprocessing changes data geometry
why transformations affect downstream analyses

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Gene means and variances

gene_summary <- tibble(
  gene = colnames(expr_var),
  mean_expression = apply(expr_var, 2, mean),
  variance = apply(expr_var, 2, var),
  sd = apply(expr_var, 2, sd)
)

head(gene_summary)


ggplot(gene_summary, aes(x = mean_expression, y = variance)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Mean expression vs variance",
    x = "Mean expression",
    y = "Variance"
  )

Instructor pause:
Ask:

Do all genes vary equally?
Why might highly variable genes dominate analyses?
Is high variance always biological?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Center data

expr_centered <- scale(expr_var, center = TRUE, scale = FALSE)

Check one gene before and after centering:

example_gene <- colnames(expr_var)[1]

mean(expr_var[, example_gene])
mean(expr_centered[, example_gene])

Instructor explanation:
Centering shifts each gene so its mean is 0.

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Scale data

expr_scaled <- scale(expr_var, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)

Check one gene before and after scaling:

sd(expr_var[, example_gene])
sd(expr_scaled[, example_gene])

Instructor explanation:
Scaling makes each gene have standard deviation 1.

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
section: Compare raw, centered, and scaled values
text:
Compare raw, centered, and scaled values

compare_gene_df <- tibble(
  cell = rownames(expr_var),
  raw = expr_var[, example_gene],
  centered = expr_centered[, example_gene],
  scaled = expr_scaled[, example_gene]
) %>%
  pivot_longer(-cell, names_to = "version", values_to = "value")

ggplot(compare_gene_df, aes(x = value)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40) +
  facet_wrap(~ version, scales = "free") +
  labs(
    title = paste("Effect of centering and scaling on", example_gene),
    x = "Value",
    y = "Count"
  )

Instructor pause:
Ask:

What changed after centering?
What changed after scaling?
Did the relative ordering of cells change for this gene?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Goal
By the end of this module, participants should understand:

why high-dimensional data is hard to visualize
PCA as a rotation of the data
PCs as directions of variation
variance explained
loadings as gene contributions

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Run PCA on unscaled data

pca_unscaled <- prcomp(expr_var, center = TRUE, scale. = FALSE)


pca_unscaled_df <- as_tibble(pca_unscaled$x[, 1:2], rownames = "cell") %>%
  left_join(
    pbmc@meta.data %>% rownames_to_column("cell"),
    by = "cell"
  )


ggplot(pca_unscaled_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = seurat_annotations)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "PCA on centered, unscaled data",
    color = "Cell type"
  )

Instructor pause:
Ask:

What structure do we see?
Are cell types separated?
What might dominate this PCA?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Run PCA on scaled data

pca_scaled <- prcomp(expr_scaled, center = FALSE, scale. = FALSE)


pca_scaled_df <- as_tibble(pca_scaled$x[, 1:2], rownames = "cell") %>%
  left_join(
    pbmc@meta.data %>% rownames_to_column("cell"),
    by = "cell"
  )


ggplot(pca_scaled_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = seurat_annotations)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "PCA on scaled data",
    color = "Cell type"
  )

Instructor pause:
Ask:

How did scaling change the PCA?
Which version is easier to interpret?
Does scaling always make things better?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
section: Compare PCA with and without labels
text:
Compare PCA with and without labels

ggplot(pca_scaled_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "PCA without cell-type labels")


ggplot(pca_scaled_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = seurat_annotations)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "PCA with cell-type labels",
    color = "Cell type"
  )

Instructor note:
This is a good place to emphasize:

Dimensionality reduction does not know the labels.
We add labels afterward to interpret the structure.

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Variance explained

variance_explained <- pca_scaled$sdev^2 / sum(pca_scaled$sdev^2)

variance_df <- tibble(
  PC = seq_along(variance_explained),
  variance_explained = variance_explained
)

ggplot(variance_df[1:30, ], aes(x = PC, y = variance_explained)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(
    title = "Variance explained by principal components",
    x = "Principal component",
    y = "Proportion of variance explained"
  )

Instructor pause:
Ask:

Why does PC1 explain the most variance?
Why do later PCs explain less?
How many PCs would you keep?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Cumulative variance explained

variance_df <- variance_df %>%
  mutate(cumulative_variance = cumsum(variance_explained))

ggplot(variance_df[1:50, ], aes(x = PC, y = cumulative_variance)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Cumulative variance explained",
    x = "Number of PCs",
    y = "Cumulative variance explained"
  )

Instructor note:
In single-cell data, variance is often spread across many PCs.
This is normal and useful to discuss.

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
PCA loadings

loadings_df <- tibble(
  gene = rownames(pca_scaled$rotation),
  PC1 = pca_scaled$rotation[, 1],
  PC2 = pca_scaled$rotation[, 2]
)

loadings_df %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(PC1))) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10)


loadings_df %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(PC2))) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10)

Instructor explanation:
Loadings tell us which genes contribute strongly to a principal component.
Instructor pause:
Ask:

If a gene has a large loading on PC1, what does that mean?
Does PCA know which genes are biologically important?
How would we interpret these genes carefully?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Goal
By the end of this module, participants should understand:

PCA, UMAP, and t-SNE are all dimensionality reduction methods
PCA emphasizes global linear structure
UMAP and t-SNE emphasize local neighborhood structure
visualizations are tools, not proof

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Use PCs as input
Instructor note:
For UMAP and t-SNE, we usually do not use all genes directly.
We use top PCs as a lower-dimensional representation of the data.

pca_coords <- pca_scaled$x[, 1:20]
dim(pca_coords)

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Run UMAP

umap_coords <- umap(
  pca_coords,
  n_neighbors = 15,
  min_dist = 0.3,
  metric = "euclidean"
)


umap_df <- as_tibble(umap_coords, .name_repair = "unique") %>%
  rename(UMAP1 = ...1, UMAP2 = ...2) %>%
  mutate(cell = rownames(pca_coords)) %>%
  left_join(
    pbmc@meta.data %>% rownames_to_column("cell"),
    by = "cell"
  )


ggplot(umap_df, aes(x = UMAP1, y = UMAP2, color = seurat_annotations)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "UMAP of PBMC3k",
    color = "Cell type"
  )

Instructor pause:
Ask:

What looks different from PCA?
Are clusters more separated?
Does more separation mean more truth?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Run t-SNE
Instructor note:
This may take a little longer. Pre-run if needed.

tsne_coords <- Rtsne(
  pca_coords,
  dims = 2,
  perplexity = 30,
  verbose = FALSE
)$Y


tsne_df <- as_tibble(tsne_coords, .name_repair = "unique") %>%
  rename(tSNE1 = ...1, tSNE2 = ...2) %>%
  mutate(cell = rownames(pca_coords)) %>%
  left_join(
    pbmc@meta.data %>% rownames_to_column("cell"),
    by = "cell"
  )


ggplot(tsne_df, aes(x = tSNE1, y = tSNE2, color = seurat_annotations)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "t-SNE of PBMC3k",
    color = "Cell type"
  )

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Compare PCA, UMAP, and t-SNE

p_pca <- ggplot(pca_scaled_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = seurat_annotations)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "PCA", color = "Cell type") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p_umap <- ggplot(umap_df, aes(x = UMAP1, y = UMAP2, color = seurat_annotations)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "UMAP", color = "Cell type") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p_tsne <- ggplot(tsne_df, aes(x = tSNE1, y = tSNE2, color = seurat_annotations)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "t-SNE", color = "Cell type")

p_pca + p_umap + p_tsne

Instructor pause:
Ask:

Which method separates clusters most clearly?
Which method is easiest to overinterpret?
Which method preserves global structure best?
Are distances between clusters meaningful in all plots?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Goal
By the end of this module, participants should understand:

analysis choices affect visual output
different parameter choices can change interpretation
plots should be interpreted critically

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Try fewer variable genes

variable_genes_200 <- VariableFeatures(pbmc)[1:200]
expr_200 <- expr_t[, variable_genes_200]
expr_200_scaled <- scale(expr_200, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)

pca_200 <- prcomp(expr_200_scaled, center = FALSE, scale. = FALSE)
pca_200_df <- as_tibble(pca_200$x[, 1:2], rownames = "cell") %>%
  left_join(
    pbmc@meta.data %>% rownames_to_column("cell"),
    by = "cell"
  )


ggplot(pca_200_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = seurat_annotations)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "PCA using top 200 variable genes",
    color = "Cell type"
  )

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Try more variable genes

variable_genes_2000 <- VariableFeatures(pbmc)[1:2000]
expr_2000 <- expr_t[, variable_genes_2000]
expr_2000_scaled <- scale(expr_2000, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)

pca_2000 <- prcomp(expr_2000_scaled, center = FALSE, scale. = FALSE)
pca_2000_df <- as_tibble(pca_2000$x[, 1:2], rownames = "cell") %>%
  left_join(
    pbmc@meta.data %>% rownames_to_column("cell"),
    by = "cell"
  )


ggplot(pca_2000_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = seurat_annotations)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "PCA using top 2000 variable genes",
    color = "Cell type"
  )

Instructor pause:
Ask:

Did the plot change?
Why would gene selection affect PCA?
Which version would you trust more, and why?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
section: UMAP with different number of PCs
text:
UMAP with different number of PCs

umap_5pcs <- umap(pca_scaled$x[, 1:5])
umap_50pcs <- umap(pca_scaled$x[, 1:50])


umap_5_df <- as_tibble(umap_5pcs, .name_repair = "unique") %>%
  rename(UMAP1 = ...1, UMAP2 = ...2) %>%
  mutate(cell = rownames(pca_scaled$x)) %>%
  left_join(
    pbmc@meta.data %>% rownames_to_column("cell"),
    by = "cell"
  )

umap_50_df <- as_tibble(umap_50pcs, .name_repair = "unique") %>%
  rename(UMAP1 = ...1, UMAP2 = ...2) %>%
  mutate(cell = rownames(pca_scaled$x)) %>%
  left_join(
    pbmc@meta.data %>% rownames_to_column("cell"),
    by = "cell"
  )


p_umap_5 <- ggplot(umap_5_df, aes(x = UMAP1, y = UMAP2, color = seurat_annotations)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "UMAP using 5 PCs") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p_umap_20 <- ggplot(umap_df, aes(x = UMAP1, y = UMAP2, color = seurat_annotations)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "UMAP using 20 PCs") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p_umap_50 <- ggplot(umap_50_df, aes(x = UMAP1, y = UMAP2, color = seurat_annotations)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "UMAP using 50 PCs")

p_umap_5 + p_umap_20 + p_umap_50

Instructor pause:
Ask:

How much did the UMAP change?
What does this tell us about parameter choices?
Why should we report preprocessing choices?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Goal
By the end of this module, participants should be able to critically evaluate dimensionality reduction plots.

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Hide labels first

ggplot(umap_df, aes(x = UMAP1, y = UMAP2)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "UMAP without labels")

Instructor activity:
Ask participants:

How many groups do you see?
Are all groups equally separated?
Do any cells appear intermediate?
What would you want to know before interpreting this?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Reveal labels

ggplot(umap_df, aes(x = UMAP1, y = UMAP2, color = seurat_annotations)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "UMAP with cell-type annotations",
    color = "Cell type"
  )

Instructor pause:
Ask:

Did the labels match expectations?
Are any cell types mixed?
Does overlap mean the labels are wrong?
Does separation mean the biology is simple?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Color by technical metrics

ggplot(umap_df, aes(x = UMAP1, y = UMAP2, color = nFeature_RNA)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "UMAP colored by number of detected genes",
    color = "nFeature_RNA"
  )


ggplot(umap_df, aes(x = UMAP1, y = UMAP2, color = percent.mt)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "UMAP colored by mitochondrial percentage",
    color = "percent.mt"
  )

Instructor note:
This is the “be careful” moment.
Emphasize:

A plot can reflect biology, technical variation, or both.

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
section: Core questions for interpreting any dimensionality reduction plot
text:
Core questions for interpreting any dimensionality reduction plot
Ask:

What are the observations?
What are the features?
What preprocessing was done?
What distance or similarity concept is implied?
What method was used?
What does the method preserve?
What labels or metadata were added after the fact?
Could technical factors explain the structure?
What follow-up analysis would you need?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Student task
Use the same PBMC3k dataset and answer:

What happens if you use 500 variable genes instead of 1000?
What happens if you run UMAP using 10 PCs instead of 20?
Which plot gives the clearest cell-type separation?
Which plot would you be most cautious about interpreting?
What preprocessing choice had the biggest visible effect?

title: Linear Algebra for Biologists
text:
Starter code

# Choose number of variable genes
n_genes <- 500

genes_use <- VariableFeatures(pbmc)[1:n_genes]

expr_use <- expr_t[, genes_use]
expr_use_scaled <- scale(expr_use, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)

pca_use <- prcomp(expr_use_scaled, center = FALSE, scale. = FALSE)

pca_use_df <- as_tibble(pca_use$x[, 1:2], rownames = "cell") %>%
  left_join(
    pbmc@meta.data %>% rownames_to_column("cell"),
    by = "cell"
  )

ggplot(pca_use_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = seurat_annotations)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.7)


# Choose number of PCs
n_pcs <- 10

umap_use <- umap(pca_use$x[, 1:n_pcs])

umap_use_df <- as_tibble(umap_use, .name_repair = "unique") %>%
  rename(UMAP1 = ...1, UMAP2 = ...2) %>%
  mutate(cell = rownames(pca_use$x)) %>%
  left_join(
    pbmc@meta.data %>% rownames_to_column("cell"),
    by = "cell"
  )

ggplot(umap_use_df, aes(x = UMAP1, y = UMAP2, color = seurat_annotations)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.7)

title: Module 2: Data transformations
text:
Before analyzing biological data, it is often necessary to preprocess and transform it. These steps can significantly change how the data is represented and how patterns appear.
What you’ll explore:

Why preprocessing is necessary for biological data

The effect of normalization, centering, and scaling

How transformations change the structure of the data

How preprocessing impacts downstream analyses

The full content for this module will be released on the morning of the workshop!
